title: Deuteronomy 12
subtitle: The One Place of Worship: Centralisation and Purity

sections.header: Verses 1–28 — Destroy the High Places; One Sanctuary

heb.gloss: the place which the LORD shall choose
heb.paragraph: The formula "the place the LORD will choose" deliberately avoids naming the location — Deuteronomy consistently uses this indirect phrase rather than specifying Jerusalem. The chosen place is defined by divine election, not human preference.

The "name theology" of Deuteronomy — God puts his name at the chosen sanctuary — is a deliberate alternative to Exodus-style presence theology. In Deuteronomy, God's name (identity, authority, character) is present at the sanctuary; the divine person is in heaven.

refs.note: "A time is coming when you will worship the Father neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem... God is spirit, and his worshipers must worship in the Spirit and in truth." Jesus radically fulfils and supersedes the Deuteronomy 12 centralisation principle: the one place of worship is no longer geographical but spiritual.

"May your eyes be open toward this temple night and day, this place of which you said, 'My Name shall be there.'" Solomon's dedication prayer uses the Deuteronomic name-theology to consecrate the Jerusalem temple.

notes.note: MacArthur: "Destroy completely all the places on the high mountains and on the hills and under every spreading tree where the nations you are dispossessing worship their gods." The command is total: no accommodation to existing religious sites. You cannot use an idol's altar and worship a different God — the form of worship shapes its content.

MacArthur: "But you are to seek the place the LORD your God will choose from among all your tribes to put his Name there for his dwelling." The contrast is deliberate: the nations worshipped wherever they chose; Israel will worship where God chooses. Worship is not a human preference but a divine appointment.

MacArthur: "Be careful not to sacrifice your burnt offerings anywhere you please. Offer them only at the place the LORD will choose." The warning against worshipping "anywhere you please" is the foundational critique of autonomous religion — worship designed by human preference rather than divine command.

MacArthur: "Be careful not to be ensnared by inquiring about their gods, saying, 'How do these nations serve their gods? We will do the same.'" Religious curiosity is not neutral — it can become religious contamination. Israel's syncretism always began with curiosity, not conversion.

craigie.source: Peter C. Craigie, The Book of Deuteronomy, NICOT (1976) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Craigie: The centralisation command is the legislative expression of Deuteronomy's monotheism: just as there is one God, there is one legitimate place to meet him. Fragmented worship sites in the ancient world typically served competing deities. Centralisation is a liturgical statement about the unity and exclusivity of Israel's God.

Craigie: The literary structure of this passage within Deuteronomy's larger address follows the standard ANE treaty pattern, moving from historical precedent to specific obligation. The rhetorical force depends on the audience recognising the covenantal framework.

tigay.source: Jeffrey H. Tigay, Deuteronomy, JPS Torah Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tigay: The permission to slaughter animals for food anywhere is a significant legal relaxation from Levitical practice (Lev 17:1–4). This reflects the practical reality of a settled, dispersed Israel — daily meat consumption cannot require a trip to the central sanctuary. The blood prohibition (v.16) remains absolute, as the sacred character of life.

Tigay: The JPS commentary highlights that this section's legislation has parallels in other ANE legal collections but is distinctively reframed within the Deuteronomic theology of covenant love and centralised worship.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The Hebrew syntax in this section reflects the characteristic Deuteronomic style — parenetic (exhortatory) prose with long conditional clauses linking obedience to blessing. The grammatical structure reinforces the theological point: covenant loyalty produces tangible outcomes.

NET Note: Several terms in this passage carry technical covenant vocabulary. The repeated use ofšāmar("to keep, guard") frames obedience as vigilant custody of God's commands, not passive acquiescence.

Calvin: On centralisation of worship: the law here is not mere legislation but divine pedagogy — God shapes his people's character through specific commands that address the conditions they will face in Canaan. Every statute reflects both God's holiness and his care for human wellbeing.

Calvin: The application extends to the church in every age. What Moses commands Israel in historical particularity, the Spirit applies to believers in principle. The form changes; the underlying demand for covenant faithfulness does not.

hist.context: Chapter 12 inaugurates the central law section (chs.12–26) with its most distinctive command: all worship must be centralised at the one sanctuary God will choose. Scattered altars and high places — the Canaanite pattern of worship at every hill and under every green tree — must be destroyed completely. The centralisation demand is simultaneously anti-syncretistic and community-forming: the whole nation gathers in one place. The chapter also addresses a practical consequence: if the slaughter of meat is decentralised, meat may be eaten without sacrifice (vv.15–16) — but blood must still not be consumed. The final verse gives the permanent anti-syncretism principle: do not worship the LORD using the methods of Canaanite religion.

ane.similarity: Both legal collections address land-based concerns: property boundaries, treatment of slaves who flee to sanctuary, levirate marriage obligations, and the distinction between intentional and accidental homicide. Both also prescribe asylum provisions for unintentional killers.
ane.difference: Hittite laws operate within a polytheistic framework with multiple divine witnesses to oaths and treaties. Deuteronomy's laws are framed as covenant requirements from Yahweh alone, with centralized worship replacing the multiple sanctuaries assumed in earlier Israelite practice.
ane.significance: The parallel shows that ancient Near Eastern cultures developed similar legal categories to address common social problems. Deuteronomy's reform of worship centralization and its covenant theology distinguish it from surrounding legal traditions.

sections.header: Verses 29–32 — No Curiosity About Canaanite Religion

heb.gloss: you shall not worship the LORD your God in their way
heb.paragraph: The prohibition of Canaanite worship methods applied to YHWH-worship is one of Deuteronomy 12's most radical statements. It is not only forbidden to worship other gods using their methods; it is also forbidden to worship the true God using those methods. Form matters.

refs.note: "They have built the high places of Baal to burn their children in the fire as offerings." Jeremiah documents exactly the syncretistic child sacrifice Moses warned against.
The sacrifices of pagans are offered to demons, not to God. You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons too. Paul's analysis of pagan sacrifice echoes the Deuteronomic concern.

notes.note: MacArthur: The logic of Deut 12:29-31 is still operative. Religious practices do not carry neutral content -- they embody and transmit theological commitments. The question "how do they worship their gods?" is always followed by "we will do the same." Israel's history from Judges through the divided kingdom is precisely the tragedy of religious imitation that Moses is anticipating.

MacArthur: "See that you do all I command you; do not add to it or take away from it." The canon-closing formula applied to the Deuteronomic law establishes the principle of sola scriptura centuries before the Reformation articulated it. Both addition (innovations in worship) and subtraction (ignoring inconvenient commands) are equally forbidden.

tigay.source: Jeffrey H. Tigay, Deuteronomy, JPS Torah Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tigay: On no curiosity about Canaanite religion: the JPS commentary observes that this legislation reflects the distinctive Deuteronomic concern for centralisation and humanitarian ethics. Where earlier law codes state the rule, Deuteronomy adds motivation clauses that ground the law in Israel's experience of God.

Tigay: Comparative ANE evidence illuminates the background — similar provisions appear in the Code of Hammurabi and the Middle Assyrian Laws, but Deuteronomy's theological framing is unique: the law serves the covenant relationship, not merely social order.

craigie.source: Peter C. Craigie, The Book of Deuteronomy, NICOT (1976) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Craigie: On no curiosity about Canaanite religion: the structure here follows the pattern of ANE treaty stipulations — specific obligations laid on the vassal by the suzerain. The parallels with Hittite treaty forms confirm that Deuteronomy's legal material is presented in a recognised international diplomatic format.

Craigie: The literary placement of this section within the larger address serves a rhetorical function — Moses builds from general principles to specific applications, a technique well attested in ancient Near Eastern legal and wisdom literature.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The Hebrew syntax in this section reflects the characteristic Deuteronomic style — parenetic (exhortatory) prose with long conditional clauses linking obedience to blessing. The grammatical structure reinforces the theological point: covenant loyalty produces tangible outcomes.

NET Note: Several terms in this passage carry technical covenant vocabulary. The repeated use ofšāmar("to keep, guard") frames obedience as vigilant custody of God's commands, not passive acquiescence.

Calvin: On no curiosity about Canaanite religion: the law here is not mere legislation but divine pedagogy — God shapes his people's character through specific commands that address the conditions they will face in Canaan. Every statute reflects both God's holiness and his care for human wellbeing.

Calvin: The application extends to the church in every age. What Moses commands Israel in historical particularity, the Spirit applies to believers in principle. The form changes; the underlying demand for covenant faithfulness does not.

hist.context: The final verses anticipate the temptation of religious imitation: after the Canaanites are driven out, Israel might adopt their worship practices for the LORD. This is explicitly forbidden. The Canaanites even burned their children to their gods (v.31), the ultimate expression of Canaanite religion's corruption and the most urgent warning about where religious syncretism ultimately leads.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Og↗ People
ppl.role: Giant king of Bashan; last of the Rephaim
ppl.text: Og king of Bashan was the last of the Rephaim — a pre-Israelite giant people. His iron bed, measuring nine cubits long (c.4.5m), was preserved in Rabbah of Ammon as a monument (Deut 3:11). Israel defeated him at Edrei after his defeat of Sihon, and Moses divided his territory among the Transjordan tribes. Deut 3 presents his defeat as proof that God can give Israel the whole land: if the giant Og fell, so will the Anakites in Canaan. He appears in numerous later biblical summaries of God’s power (Ps 135, 136; Neh 9).

src.title: Hittite Suzerainty Treaties (14th–13th c. BC)
src.quote: Klaus Baltzer (1960) and Meredith Kline (1963) demonstrated that Deuteronomy follows the six-part structure of Hittite vassal treaties: preamble (1:1–5), historical prologue (1:6–3:29), stipulations (4–26), document clause (27:1–9), witnesses (31:19–22), and blessings/curses (28). This structure is attested only in 14th–13th century BC treaties, strongly supporting Mosaic authorship.
src.note: The treaty-form evidence is the most powerful external argument for Deuteronomy’s early date. Subsequent Assyrian vassal treaties (7th c. BC) have a different form, making a late (Josian) dating of the book structurally improbable.

src.title: Vassal Treaties of Esarhaddon (672 BC)
src.quote: The Neo-Assyrian VTE contains curse language strikingly similar to Deut 28, leading some scholars (Weinfeld, Steymans) to argue Deuteronomy was modelled on Assyrian treaties. However, the VTE represents only the curse section; Deuteronomy’s full six-part structure matches only Hittite treaties.
src.note: The parallel demonstrates that ancient Near Eastern covenant forms were widespread, but the direction of dependence is disputed. The Hittite parallel is structurally closer and temporally earlier, supporting the traditional dating.

src.title: Ugaritic and Ancient Near Eastern Covenant Terminology
src.quote: The vocabulary of Deuteronomy —berît(covenant),ahavah(love as covenant loyalty),hesed(loyal love), ‘adon’ (lord) — is attested in Ugaritic and other ANE diplomatic texts as standard covenant terminology.
src.note: This shows that Deuteronomy’s language of love, loyalty, and service was not merely personal piety but carried legal-diplomatic weight: Israel’s relationship with God is structured as a formal suzerainty covenant.

rec.who: Origen, Homilies on Deuteronomy (3rd c. AD)
rec.text: "Origen read Deuteronomy christologically throughout, interpreting Moses’ farewell discourses as prefiguring Christ’s teaching, the “prophet like Moses” (18:15) as a direct prophecy of Christ, and the land as a figure of the soul’s heavenly inheritance."

"MacArthur: "Destroy completely all the places on the high mountains and on the hills and under every spreading tree where the nations you are dispossessing worship their gods." The command is total: n…"

"They have built the high places of Baal to burn their children in the fire as offerings." Jeremiah documents exactly the syncretistic child sacrifice Moses warned against.

tx.title: LXX Deuteronomy and the MT divergences
tx.content: MTandLXXDeuteronomy diverge more significantly than other Pentateuchal books. The LXX adds explanatory expansions, especially in the blessings and curses (ch.28) and the Song of Moses (ch.32).
tx.note: The LXX Deuteronomy was translated with greater theological freedom than other books. It often interprets rather than translates, and has shaped the NT’s use of Deuteronomy (Paul cites LXX Deut in Rom 10 and Gal 3).

tx.title: Qumran and the Samaritan Pentateuch
tx.content: Deuteronomy is the most-copied book at Qumran (29 manuscripts), showing its centrality to Second Temple Judaism. TheSPDeuteronomy adds a tenth commandment mandating an altar on Gerizim (Exod 20:17b insert), the central SP polemic.
tx.note: The Nash Papyrus (c.150–100 BC) preserves the Decalogue and Shema from Deuteronomy — the oldest surviving Hebrew manuscript of any biblical text. It shows a mixed text-type combining MT and LXX features.

positions.name: Critical scholarship: Josian composition (de Wette, Wellhausen)
positions.proponents: The dominant critical view since de Wette (1805) identifies the “book of the law” found in 2 Kgs 22 with Deuteronomy, placing its composition in Josiah’s reign (c.621 BC). Deuteronomy on this view was written to legitimise Josiah’s centralisation reforms.
positions.argument: This view treats the Mosaic attribution as a literary fiction and explains the book’s similarities with 7th-century Assyrian treaty forms — though the Hittite parallels are structurally closer.

positions.name: Mosaic authorship / early composition (Kline, Craigie, evangelical scholarship)
positions.proponents: The Hittite suzerainty treaty structure (attested only in 14th–13th c. BC) strongly supports early composition. The book’s internal claims (1:1, 31:9,22,24) and NT citations (Jesus cites Deuteronomy three times in the temptation: Matt 4:4,7,10) support Mosaic origin.
positions.argument: Craigie and Kline argue that the treaty-form evidence makes a late date structurally implausible. The “prophet like Moses” passage (18:15) is best read as a genuine prediction, not a post-exilic retrojection.

themes.note: The One Place of Worship: Centralisation and Purity

coaching.tip: 'You must not worship the LORD your God in their way' (v. 4). Centralized worship—one sanctuary, not many high places. This distinguished Israel from Canaanite practice. Location mattered; God chose where to be worshiped.

Distance from the sanctuary permitted secular slaughter (vv. 20-25). All meat wasn't sacrifice; some was just food. The concession acknowledged reality: not everyone lived near the chosen place. Holiness accommodated geography.
